1: David flees from Naioth in Ramah and comes to Jonathan, asking: 'What have I done? What is my sin against your father, that he seeks my life?' The question is genuine — David does not know what specific act triggered the latest assault. His appeal to Jonathan reflects the covenant they share: Jonathan is both the one who can intercede with Saul and the one who knows Saul's inner state.
2: Jonathan's response reflects incomplete information: 'You shall not die. My father does nothing without disclosing it to me. Why should my father hide this from me? It is not so.' Jonathan believes he is in Saul's confidence. His assumption — that Saul would have told him about any serious plan against David — reflects the normal expectation of a crown prince's access to royal decision-making. He is wrong, and David knows it.
3: David swears: 'Your father knows well that I have found favor in your eyes, and he thinks, Do not let Jonathan know this, lest he be grieved. But truly, as YHWH lives and as you live, there is but a step between me and death.' The phrase 'a step between me and death' (kəpeṣaʿ bênî ûḇên hammāweṯ) became a proverbial expression of mortal danger. David's assessment is accurate: he is not catastrophizing but calculating.
4: Jonathan offers David whatever he wants. The offer reflects the latitude Jonathan has as crown prince — he can act on David's behalf in ways a lower-ranking person could not. The covenant between them creates a mutual obligation structure: David's request will come with a corresponding pledge.
5: David proposes the new moon festival test. The new moon (ḥōḏeš) was a significant monthly festival in ancient Israel requiring the sounding of trumpets, special sacrifices, and, in the court context, a formal meal at the king's table (Numbers 10:10; 1 Samuel 28:11-15). David's absence from the new moon feast would be conspicuous — a court official's failure to attend was either a medical emergency or a political signal. The king's reaction to David's absence would reveal his intent.
6: David's cover story: if Saul asks, Jonathan is to say David requested to go to Bethlehem for the family's annual sacrifice. The annual family sacrifice at Bethlehem is a plausible claim — local religious festivals at ancestral sites were normal practice. Jesse's family in Bethlehem would have an annual commemoration around the new moon or a specified religious festival. The story is credible enough to require Saul to choose between accepting it and revealing his hand.
7: 'If he says, Good, your servant is well; but if he is angry, then know that harm is determined by him.' David's request is for a binary diagnostic: Saul's reaction to a credible excuse will reveal his pre-decided intentions. If Saul accepts the excuse, David's position may be recoverable; if Saul's anger flares regardless, his intention is certain. This is the same empirical-test logic the Philistine priests applied to the ark-return (1 Samuel 6:9).
8: 'Show covenant loyalty (ḥesed) to your servant, for you have brought your servant into a covenant of YHWH with you.' David invokes the specific covenant term ḥesed — loyalty, steadfast love, the covenant quality of faithfulness to obligations. He situates the test within their formal covenant relationship: Jonathan owes David covenant loyalty because they have formally bound themselves to each other before YHWH. The appeal is legal, not merely emotional.
9: Jonathan assures David that if he knew his father intended harm, he would tell him. The covenant-friendship here is reciprocal: Jonathan is not merely acting as a favor to David but fulfilling obligations he has voluntarily undertaken. His assurance — 'the LORD do so to Jonathan and more also if it please my father to do you harm, and I do not disclose it to you' — is a self-imprecatory oath, the strongest form of commitment available.
10: David asks: 'Who will tell me if your father answers harshly?' The practical question is how to communicate the outcome securely. A direct face-to-face meeting after the new moon would be dangerous if Saul intends harm. The arrow-signal system (vv35-40) is Jonathan's solution — a covert communication method that looks like a training exercise.
11: Jonathan takes David out into the field. The field setting for covenant-making is a liminal space — away from walls that could have ears, outside the domestic and political structures. The covenant they are about to renew was originally made in 18:3 and renewed in 1 Samuel 23:18; this is the fullest articulation of its terms.
12: Jonathan invokes YHWH as witness: 'YHWH, God of Israel! When I have sounded out my father, about this time tomorrow or the third day, behold, if he is well disposed toward David, shall I not then send to you and disclose it to you?' The divine witness to the covenant communication plan is the formal covenant structure: YHWH will hold Jonathan accountable to deliver the message faithfully.
13: 'But should it please my father to do you harm, the LORD do so to Jonathan and more also if I do not disclose it to you and send you away, that you may go in safety. May YHWH be with you, as he has been with my father.' Jonathan's farewell blessing — 'may YHWH be with you as he has been with my father' — is a remarkable acknowledgment: Jonathan already knows that YHWH is departing from his father and going with David. The blessing passes YHWH's presence from the Saulide line to David.
14: Jonathan asks David to show him ḥesed YHWH — YHWH-quality covenant loyalty — if David is still alive when YHWH cuts off David's enemies. The request anticipates David's eventual kingship and Jonathan's either death or subordination. Jonathan is negotiating the terms of David's future treatment of his descendants from a position of vulnerability, acknowledging that dynastic transitions in the ANE typically involved elimination of the former dynasty's heirs.
15: 'And do not cut off your covenant loyalty from my house forever, even when YHWH cuts off every one of the enemies of David from the face of the earth.' Jonathan uses the language of cutting off (kāraṯ) — the same term used for covenant-making ('cutting a covenant') — and applies it to his own potential fate. The covenant he has cut with David is the guarantee against the cutting off his descendants might otherwise face. This covenant will be fulfilled in David's treatment of Mephibosheth (2 Sam 9).
16: Jonathan cuts a covenant with the house of David. The formal covenant act creates a legally binding obligation on David and his successors toward Jonathan's descendants. The covenant is with the house of David (not just David personally) — it extends across generations, acknowledging that Jonathan's children will be alive in David's kingdom long after both men are dead.
17: Jonathan makes David swear again in his love for him — 'for he loved him as he loved his own soul' (kəʾahaḇaṯ napšô). The renewal of the oath in the context of love is the emotional core of what has been legally structured in the preceding verses. The covenant is not a cold contract but a formalization of genuine friendship of the deepest ANE kind — the love that exceeds self-interest.
18: Jonathan sets out the arrow-signal plan for communicating after the new moon. 'You will be missed because your seat will be empty.' The social significance of the empty seat in court — an assigned position at the royal table whose absence is both visually and politically conspicuous — is the mechanism of the test. Presence at the royal table was both an honor and an obligation for court officials.
19: Jonathan instructs David to go to the place where he hid on the day of the matter and stay beside the stone Ezel. The stone Ezel is an apparently known landmark near Gibeah, mentioned only here. The specificity of the hiding place — the same place used during a previous emergency — suggests an established pattern of covert meeting, consistent with the secret nature of the Jonathan-David relationship under Saul's surveillance.
20: Jonathan's arrow signal: he will shoot three arrows to the side of the stone as if shooting at a mark. The use of archery practice as a cover for the signal is practical intelligence tradecraft — it provides a visible and plausible reason for being in the field with a servant, while the actual message is encoded in the directional command given to the servant.
21: 'If I say to the boy, Look, the arrows are on this side of you, take them — then you are to come, for it is safe for you and there is no danger, as YHWH lives.' The 'come' signal is the favorable response: David can safely return to Gibeah. The invocation of YHWH even for the signal phrase — 'as YHWH lives' — grounds the communication system in covenant reliability.
22: 'But if I say to the boy, Look, the arrows are beyond you — then go, for YHWH has sent you away.' The 'beyond' signal means flight is necessary. Jonathan frames even the negative outcome as YHWH's action: 'YHWH has sent you away.' Whether the news is stay or go, Jonathan situates it within divine providence. The covenant between them has YHWH as its guarantor and interpreter.
23: 'And as for the matter of which you and I have spoken, behold, YHWH is between you and me forever.' The covenant's ultimate grounding is YHWH's permanent witness. The phrase 'YHWH bênî ûḇêneḵā ʿaḏ-ʿôlām' — YHWH between you and me forever — is the covenant's theological capstone: it transcends the immediate crisis, their lifetimes, and extends to their descendants (cf. v14-15).
24: David hides in the field. At the new moon Saul sits at the feast to eat, seated at his usual place against the wall — the king's position with his back protected. Jonathan sits opposite and Abner, Saul's commander, beside Saul. David's place is empty. The meal scene is carefully rendered: the exact positions of the diners are noted, making David's absence conspicuous by its specificity.
25: On the first day Saul says nothing. The silence is strategic — Saul may be giving David one day's grace, or he may be calculating. The text only records that he thought 'Something has happened to him; he is not clean, surely he is not clean.' Ritual uncleanness (ṭumʾāh) from nocturnal emission or contact with a corpse was a legitimate reason for missing a cultic meal (Leviticus 15:16-18). Saul's first assumption is generous or at least plausible.
26: This verse records Saul's first-day silence. His initial assumption of ritual uncleanness is the most charitable available interpretation of David's absence. The cultic calendar and purity requirements provided a ready-made cover for absences that might otherwise be suspicious — Saul's invocation of it suggests either genuine consideration or a one-day grace period before pressing the matter.
27: On the second day — when David's place is again empty — Saul asks Jonathan directly why Jesse's son has not come to the feast 'yesterday or today.' The phrase 'Jesse's son' rather than 'David' is Saul's distancing language — he uses the patronymic to signal that David is not in his estimation a member of the court family but an outsider whose absence is a political statement.
28: Jonathan delivers the cover story: David urgently requested to go to his family's sacrifice in Bethlehem at his brother's command. The elaboration — 'commanded by my brother' — adds familial authority to the request, making it appear that David had no choice but to comply. The story is plausible and specific enough that Saul must either accept it or reveal that he has pre-decided to harm David regardless of the explanation.
29: 'Please let me go, for our clan holds a sacrifice in the city, and my brother has commanded me to be there.' The appeal to family obligation and prior commitment is designed to be unassailable within the honor-culture of Iron Age Israel: refusing a family sacrificial command from an elder brother would be a social offense. Saul's reaction will reveal whether he accepts the cultural logic or is operating outside it.
30: Saul's anger flares against Jonathan: 'You son of a perverse and rebellious woman!' The social insult — directed at Jonathan's mother rather than at Jonathan himself — is the ANE equivalent of the most severe personal attack. Insulting a man through his mother's virtue and loyalty was among the worst forms of verbal aggression in ancient Near Eastern cultures, documented in both Mesopotamian and Ugaritic texts. Saul's rage bypasses rational argument and goes directly to social humiliation.
31: 'For as long as Jesse's son lives on the earth, neither you nor your kingship will be established.' Saul's explicit statement reveals what he has known and feared: Jonathan's kingship succession depends on David's elimination. Saul has recognized the threat to his dynasty's future that David represents — not because David is actively plotting against it, but because YHWH's designation of David has created an alternative dynastic claim that Saul cannot eliminate through normal political means.
32: Jonathan protests: 'Why should he be put to death? What has he done?' Jonathan continues to argue David's innocence — the same argument he made in 1 Samuel 19:4-5. His persistence in defending David even under direct royal anger demonstrates the depth of his covenant commitment.
33: Saul hurls his spear at Jonathan to strike him — the same violent reaction he had toward David (1 Samuel 18:11; 1 Samuel 19:10). The spear-throw at his own son is the chapter's decisive moment: Saul has now crossed the line from political violence against a rival to familial violence against his heir. Jonathan knows from this that Saul 'was determined to kill David' — the resolution is definitive, not conditional.
34: Jonathan rises from the table in fierce anger and eats nothing on the second day of the new moon. He is grieved for David because his father had shamed him. Jonathan's grief is double: the covenant relationship with David is severed by his father's decision, and the shaming of Jonathan himself (the spear-throw in public) is a political and personal humiliation. He fasts involuntarily from grief.
35: In the morning Jonathan goes out to the field for the appointed signal, and a small boy is with him. The elaborate preparation for a simple message — a boy, arrows, a designated field — is the intelligence operation's final execution. The boy is a cover; the arrows are the medium; the directional command is the message.
36: Jonathan says to the boy: 'Run and find the arrows that I shoot.' As the boy runs, Jonathan shoots an arrow beyond him. The 'beyond' placement of the arrow is the dangerous-departure signal. The boy runs to the arrow's location without understanding the significance of where Jonathan shot.
37: When the boy comes to where Jonathan's arrow fell, Jonathan calls after the boy: 'Is not the arrow beyond you?' The question encodes the message; David, hidden among the rocks nearby, hears it and understands. The signal system works precisely as designed: the servant receives the surface instruction; David receives the covert message.
38: Jonathan calls after the boy: 'Hurry, be quick, do not stay.' The urgency of the additional commands — beyond what was needed for simple arrow-retrieval — reinforces the departure signal. Jonathan is pressing the message through the surface action.
39: The boy knows nothing. Only Jonathan and David understood the matter. The narrative's note about the servant's ignorance confirms the system's security: the covert communication remained concealed even from the immediate participant. This is the characteristic of a well-designed signal system.
40: Jonathan gives his weapons to the boy and sends him into the city. The sending away of the servant creates privacy for the farewell. Without the witness, the covenant friends can drop the cover and speak plainly. Jonathan's weapons — the bow and arrows just used in the signal — are sent away as if the training exercise is complete.
41: As soon as the boy is gone, David comes out from beside the south stone, falls on his face and bows three times. Then they kiss each other and weep together, David weeping the most. The formal greeting (prostration and bowing) followed by kissing and weeping is the full social-emotional range of the ANE farewell — the prostration honors the covenant superior (Jonathan as crown prince), the kiss seals the covenant bond, and the weeping acknowledges the finality of the parting. This is the last time they will see each other alive.
42: Jonathan says: 'Go in peace, because we have sworn both of us in the name of YHWH, saying, YHWH shall be between me and you, and between my offspring and your offspring, forever.' Jonathan's final words are not personal sentiment but covenantal declaration — the covenant itself, with its trans-generational scope, is his farewell gift. David then rises and leaves, and Jonathan goes into the city. The men depart in opposite directions; the narrative has given them everything but proximity.
